articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 24, 1914. Biographical sketch of Rev. William Douglass (b. Baltimore, Sept. 6, 1805), pupil of Daniel Coker, who came to Philadelphia in 1843 as rector of St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church after John F. Cook, James Needham and Robert Gordon proposed him; his wife was a daughter of Hezekiah Grice. Bolivar places his arrival amid the generation of James Forten, Robert Bogle, Bishop Richard Allen, Robert Douglass, John C. Bowers, Bishop Morris Brown, Junius C. Morrell, Robert Douglass Jr., William Whipper, Robert Purvis, Frederick Hinton, Stephen Gloucester and Charles Gardner.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 16, 1912. History of Liberty Hall, 716-718 Lombard St., home of the Institute for Colored Youth 1854-1865 and purchased in 1866 by public-spirited men (William Whipper, William Still, Rev. Stephen Smith, Robert Adger, Henry Jones, Bishop J. J. Clinton, Rev. Elisha Weaver, William Warrick, James W. Purnell). Recalls ICY teachers Charles L. Reason, E. D. Bassett, Sarah M. Douglass, Robert Campbell, Grace A. Mapps, Jacob C. White Jr. and Octavius V. Catto, the Pythian Club and Banneker Institute rooms, and Fifteenth Amendment meetings held in the hall.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 15, 1913. Links Charleston and Philadelphia: the 1822 Denmark Vesey uprising drove Morris Brown Sr. to Philadelphia, where he joined Bishop Richard Allen and became second bishop of the A.M.E. Church. Also recounts a c. 1858-59 affair in which William Still, Robert Adger, Mrs. Carr and young men of the Phoenix Club (922 South St.) spirited a fugitive slave to Canada and plotted to tar and feather a visiting Charleston slaveholder named Francis, who was hurried out of the city.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 27, 1912. On Pennsylvania's $20,000 appropriation for the semi-centennial of Emancipation, with Harry W. Bass, first Black member of the state legislature, among the commissioners. Bolivar notes that 'as far back as 1838 there was a big work projected, engaged in and consummated, to show the industrial, educational, religious and material position' of the people (the 1838 census/register of Black Philadelphia), an 1851 handicraft exhibition at the Philadelphia Institute, and the collecting of William H. Dorsey, Robert M. Adger and Jacob C. White for the American Negro Historical Society.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 19, 1913. Retrospect of Philadelphia's great celebration of April 26, 1871 for the Fifteenth Amendment, promoted by Joseph C. Bustill with ward committees; some participants had been Pennsylvania voters before the 1837-38 disfranchisement. Workers included William Whipper, Henry Jones, Thomas J. Dorsey, Revs. Stephen Smith and John B. Reeve, Jacob C. White Jr., Isaiah C. Wear and Octavius V. Catto; orators at Horticultural Hall included Robert Purvis, Isaiah C. Wear, William Howard Day and Catto.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 20, 1914. History of organs and church music: the first pipe organ in a colored church installed at St. Thomas' in 1833, built by J. C. Stanbridge and played by Miss Ann Appo, sister of musicians William and Joseph Appo (members of the Walnut Street Theatre orchestra in 1826); John C. Bowers the second organist, playing forty years in one church; Henry Williams composed the ballad 'Lorette'; Rev. Charles Gardner instituted sacred song recitals at First African Presbyterian with players from Frank Johnson's band under E. D. Roland, leading to 'The Creation' sung in 1840-41 with a chorus of 150; organ builder Joseph Carter later trained Robert M. Adger.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 2, 1914. Survey of Philadelphia's Black newspapers of 1889-1896: The Echo (L. J. Coppin, William T. Simpson, Abel P. Caldwell), The Sun (G. W. Gardiner, William H. Heard), The Standard-Echo, The Sunday Journal (Robert G. Still) and the Sunday Herald (Thomas Wallace Swann) — drawn from the anniversary number of The Echo, May 1889, its articles, advertisers and its notice of a competitive concert at the Academy of Music.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 23, 1912. On English abolitionist George Thompson's visit to Philadelphia (c. 1834-35): entertained socially by James Forten, with meetings organized by the young men John C. Bowers, James McC. Crummill, Robert Purvis, Frederick Hinton, Junius C. Morrell, James Needham, William Whipper and James C. Matthews. Thompson toured the churches, schools and businesses of free Black Philadelphia and was later assisted in Britain by Dr. James McCune Smith; his picture survives in the Adger collection at the Home for Aged and Infirm Colored Persons.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 4, 1914. History of Black newspapers: Freedom's Journal and Rights of All, founded and edited by John B. Russwurm (1827); Philadelphia's Demosthenian Shield published by Benjamin Stanley in 1840; William Whipper's magazine The Moral Reformer (1837), called the city's first; the Anglo-African of 1859 with contributor Parker T. Smith; and later Philadelphia journalists and the Daily Advocate of 1877 (S. L. Nichols and Henry H. Griffin) with its many advertisers, including Robert M. Adger's furniture store under Liberty Hall.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 24, 1912. Early Black baseball: the Excelsiors of Philadelphia (spring 1866, promoted by James H. Francis, James F. Needham and Francis Wood) and the Pythians (sponsored by Octavius V. Catto, Jacob C. White Jr., David Knight and J. Whipper Purnell), with games against the Bachelors of Albany, Washington's Mutuals and Alerts, and the Uniques of Chicago; Catto captained the Pythians with consummate skill.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 1, 1913. Critique of the 1913 Emancipation Exposition at Broad and Oregon, contrasted with earlier self-organized efforts: the c. 1851 handicraft exhibition showing furniture from the workshops of Duterte, Baptist, LeCount, Clement, Grinage and Charnock; clothing from Gailberry, Moore, Bowers, Depee and Ayers; printing and dental appliances by J. McC. Crummill; daguerreotypes by Richardson; sail and awning work by Bolivar and Anthony; and the Fifteenth Amendment Jubilee of 1871, all funded and managed by the people themselves.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 21, 1914 (continuation). Quotes Samuel Ringgold Ward's autobiography, including the anecdote of a daughter of James Forten translating a French newspaper at the Forten home on Lombard below Fourth for a Colonizationist visitor who that evening denied Black intellectual capacity. Reprints John Greenleaf Whittier's hitherto-uncollected poem 'To the Daughters of James Forten,' preserved and communicated by Charlotte Forten Grimké, granddaughter of James Forten and wife of Rev. Francis J. Grimké.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 30, 1914. History of 'Big' Wesley Church, Lombard near Fifth (est. c. 1830): Bishop Richard Allen lived a few doors west; Robert Johnson taught sight-singing there, and with Morris Brown was trained by John Cromwell in 1821-22; neighbors included Robert Purvis's mother, Union Baptist under Rev. Daniel Scott, the Clarksons' schools, Elizabeth Redding's boarding house and Dr. James J. G. Bias, capper and leecher. Recalls the 1842 'Bias mob' riot against the West Indian emancipation parade, when Benjamin Welsh carried a banner to safety and the wounded were treated in the guarded church; its walls rang with the eloquence of Robert Purvis, John C. Bowers, William Whipper, Isaiah C. Wear, James McC. Crummill, Charles L. Remond, Samuel R. Ward, Henry Highland Garnet and Frederick Douglass.

events.name: George Thompson's anti-slavery visit to Philadelphia
events.description: English abolitionist George Thompson, drawn by the annual conventions, was entertained socially by James Forten; the young men of the day got up meetings and proffered social amenities in his honor (recalled by W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 23, 1912).

events.name: West Indian emancipation parade and 'Bias mob' riot
events.description: Parade commemorating British West Indian emancipation, held in defiance of threats, attacked by a mob (the 'Bias mob,' named for Dr. James J. G. Bias). Benjamin Welsh carried a banner to safety; the wounded were treated in Wesley Church, which was guarded against threatened burning (Bolivar, Tribune, May 30, 1914).

events.name: Call of Rev. William Douglass to St. Thomas African Episcopal Church
events.description: After the death of founder Absalom Jones (1818) left the parish without clergy, a conversation among John F. Cook, James Needham and Robert Gordon led to inviting William Douglass of Baltimore, newly ordained to the diaconate by Bishop Stone, to the rectorship; he came to the city in 1843 (Bolivar, Tribune, Oct. 24, 1914).

events.name: First pipe organ installed at St. Thomas' Church
events.location: St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church, Philadelphia
events.description: The first pipe organ in any colored church of the city, built by J. C. Stanbridge; the first player was Miss Ann Appo, sister of musicians William and Joseph Appo (Bolivar, Tribune, Jun. 20, 1914).

events.name: Performance of 'The Creation' by sacred-music chorus
events.description: Sacred song recitals instituted by Rev. Charles Gardner nearly seventy-five years before 1914, assisted by an orchestra drawn from Frank Johnson's band with E. D. Roland as leader, culminating in 'The Creation' sung in 1840-41 with principals, a chorus of 150 and an orchestra of 50 (Bolivar, Tribune, Jun. 20, 1914).

events.name: Phoenix Club rescue of a fugitive from Charleston
events.description: A runaway slave visiting from Canadian shelter was recognized by a Charleston slaveholder named Francis boarding at the Stills'; Mrs. Carr sent him in disguise to Robert Adger, who spirited him toward Canada. A council at the Phoenix Club, 922 South St., prepared to tar and feather Francis before Adger and William Still got him away safely (Bolivar, Tribune, Feb. 15, 1913).

events.name: Purchase and remodeling of Liberty Hall (old ICY building)
events.description: A band of public-spirited men purchased the old Institute for Colored Youth building to give the community a much-needed hall, remodeled with stores, a 500-seat main hall, and rooms used by the Banneker Institute and Pythian Club (Bolivar, Tribune, Nov. 16, 1912).

events.name: Fifteenth Amendment celebration parade and jubilee
events.description: Citywide celebration of the Fifteenth Amendment, promoted and managed by Joseph C. Bustill, funded by the colored people themselves, with ward committees, a long procession, and speaking, music and banquet at Horticultural Hall (Bolivar, Tribune, Apr. 19, 1913).
